It means that after processing the input signals in the whole network, the artificial neural network receives a final output which in the case of supervised machine learning can be compared with the desired (actual label) output.
glossary.sentence: Learning in artificial neural networks is related to modifying weights in some particular way . It means that after processing the input signals in the whole network, the artificial neural network receives a final output which in the case of supervised machine learning can be compared with the desired (actual label) output.

glossary.definition: A learning rule is applied to modify the weights of the network if there is a difference between the desired output and the output of the network.
glossary.sentence: In case if there is a difference between the desired output and the output of the network, a learning rule is applied to modify the weights of the network. Therefore, the artificial neural network starts the next processing step with updated weight values.

glossary.definition: Artificial neural networks are resistant to partially or entirely damaged processing units or connections between processing units and network operation changes very little. This is called fault tolerance, or graceful degradation, and it is explained by the fact that the knowledge of the network is not stored in one place but distributed all over the network.
glossary.sentence: According to ( Borisovs et al., 1998), at the same time, artificial neural networks are resistant to partially or entirely damaged processing units or connections between processing units and network operation changes very little. It is called fault tolerance, or, in other words, graceful degradation, and it is explained by the fact that the knowledge of the network is not stored in one place, but it is distributed all over the network ( Borisovs et al., 1998).

glossary.definition: Pattern recognition assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs; pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data;

glossary.definition: Prediction involves tasks such as identifying a disease from symptoms, causes from effects.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs; pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data; prediction, such as identifying a disease from symptoms, causes from effects;

glossary.definition: Optimization involves finding the 'best' organization of constraints.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs; pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data; prediction, such as identifying a disease from symptoms, causes from effects; optimization;

glossary.definition: Noise cancellation involves separating signals from noise.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs; pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data; prediction, such as identifying a disease from symptoms, causes from effects; optimization; noise cancellation;

glossary.definition: Function approximation involves approximating complex relationships between inputs and outputs.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs; pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data; prediction, such as identifying a disease from symptoms, causes from effects; optimization; noise cancellation; function approximation;

glossary.definition: Time series prediction involves predicting future values based on past values.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs; pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data; prediction, such as identifying a disease from symptoms, causes from effects; optimization; noise cancellation; function approximation; time series prediction;

glossary.definition: Control involves managing and regulating systems.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs; pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data; prediction, such as identifying a disease from symptoms, causes from effects; optimization; noise cancellation; function approximation; time series prediction; control;

glossary.definition: A model developed by Rosenblatt in the late 1950s.
glossary.sentence: Rosenblatt developed the perceptron model in the late 1950s (Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: The simplest and commonly known type of artificial neural network with limited practical applicability due to its ability to learn only linearly separable functions.
glossary.sentence: A Single Layer Perceptron is the simplest and the commonly known type of artificial neural network, but its practical applicability is limited because it can learn only linearly separable functions.

glossary.definition: The only processing layer in a single layer perceptron, emphasizing that all inputs are connected directly to the outputs.
glossary.sentence: The title of the network – “a single layer perceptron” – emphasizes that the network has only one processing layer; all the inputs are connected directly to the outputs.

glossary.definition: A perceptron network with multiple outputs is considered as m separate networks because each weight affects only one of the outputs, resulting in m separate training processes.
glossary.sentence: the perceptron network with m outputs is really m separate networks because each weight affects only one of the outputs; thus, there will be m separate training processes.

glossary.definition: The process by which the perceptron learns the best possible values for weights to correctly classify data through small adjustments after each training example.
glossary.sentence: The perceptron learns the best possible values for weights that will allow correctly to classify the data (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: Adjustments made to the weights of the perceptron after each training example to reduce the difference between the actual and desired output.
glossary.sentence: the perceptron adjusts the weights; otherwise, if the predicted output is the same as the actual output, then the adjustments of the weights are not made because the performance of the perceptron network is considered to be satisfactory;

glossary.definition: The method used for computing weight correction in the perceptron.
glossary.sentence: The weight correction is usually computed by applying the delta rule :

glossary.definition: Criteria that can stop the learning process, such as reaching a predefined maximum permissible error or achieving a predefined number of iterations.
glossary.sentence: the perceptron continues the learning process (Steps 2-5) for other examples in the training set until some termination criterion is reached.

glossary.definition: One cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all data objects in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes.
glossary.sentence: An epoch represents one cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all of the data objects contained in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes.

glossary.definition: The sum of squared differences between actual and predicted outputs for all output neurons and all training examples.
glossary.sentence: The total error of the network is calculated as the sum of squared differences between the actual and predicted outputs for all output neurons for all training examples.

glossary.definition: A basic type of feed-forward neural network with greater processing power, capable of representing any continuous function with one hidden layer and discontinuous functions with two hidden layers.
glossary.sentence: The multilayer perceptron is a basic type of feed-forward neural network that, with one hidden layer, can represent any continuous function of the input signals and with two hidden layers - even discontinuous functions ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A type of neural network with greater processing power that can model practically any function of arbitrary complexity.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, a feed-forward neural network has greater processing power, and it can model practically any function of arbitrary complexity, where the number of inputs and the number of hidden layers determine the function complexity (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: A layer in a multilayer perceptron consisting of computing neurons that usually perform feature detection. Neurons in this layer cannot be observed through input/output behavior.
glossary.sentence: at least one hidden layer that consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features.

glossary.definition: Values in a dataset that are used as inputs to the neural network.
glossary.sentence: Imagine that we have a dataset that contains values for two input features.

glossary.definition: A task where the goal is to classify data into two classes.
glossary.sentence: we want to use the neural network for the binary classification task.

glossary.definition: A component in a neural network that calculates the weighted sum and applies an activation function.
glossary.sentence: Each processing unit calculates the weighted sum, including the bias input, and then applies the activation function.

glossary.definition: based on assigning initial random values to weights, selecting a training example from the training set, performing feed-forwarding and backpropagation of error backwards in the network until some termination criterion is achieved.
glossary.sentence: The learning in multilayer perceptron is based on the following steps (Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011): assigning initial random values to weights; selecting a training example from the training set; performing feed-forwarding – propagation of inputs forward in the network; performing backpropagation of error backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer; the training process continues until some termination criterion is achieved (see above). Therefore, the learning in the multilayer perceptron is based on the backpropagation algorithm .

glossary.definition: includes two main phases: feed-forwarding and backpropagation.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the learning in the multilayer perceptron is based on the backpropagation algorithm . It includes two main phases: feed-forwarding and backpropagation (Fig. The inputs are propagated over the network from the input layer to the output layer during the feed-forwarding .

glossary.definition: propagation of inputs forward in the network from the input layer to the output layer.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the learning in the multilayer perceptron is based on the backpropagation algorithm . It includes two main phases: feed-forwarding and backpropagation (Fig. The inputs are propagated over the network from the input layer to the output layer during the feed-forwarding .

glossary.definition: propagation of error backwards in the network from the output layer to the input layer.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the learning in the multilayer perceptron is based on the backpropagation algorithm . It includes two main phases: feed-forwarding and backpropagation (Fig. The inputs are propagated over the network from the input layer to the output layer during the feed-forwarding .

glossary.definition: a dataset contains data objects together with the right output labels, meaning that by feeding a particular data object into the network, we expect to acquire a known output.
glossary.sentence: In a supervised machine learning task, the dataset contains data objects together with the right output labels. It means that by feeding a particular data object into the network, we expect to acquire a known output.

glossary.definition: the difference between the right output and the predicted output.
glossary.sentence: However, in reality, the output predicted by the network differs from the actual output. Thus, there is a difference between the right output and the predicted output. It is called an error.

glossary.definition: units responsible for some fraction of the error in each of the output units; their values are not specified by the training data.
glossary.sentence: The training data do not say what value the hidden processing units should have, and each hidden processing unit is responsible for some fraction of the error in each of the output units (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: modifying weights in all network layers through the propagation of the error from the output layer to the input layer.
glossary.sentence: The backpropagation algorithm allows modifying weights in all network layers through the propagation of the error from the output layer to the input layer (Fig.

glossary.term: types of errors used by the backpropagation algorithm
glossary.definition: the first is for output neurons, and the second is only for neurons in hidden layers.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm uses two types of errors: the first is used for output neurons, and the second is used only for neurons in hidden layers.

glossary.definition: calculated using the predicted output and the real class label.
glossary.sentence: In the output neuron, we know the predicted output and the real class label and, thus, can calculate the error in the following way:

glossary.definition: calculated based on the contribution of each hidden processing unit to the total error.
glossary.sentence: In the hidden layers, the error for each processing unit is calculated in the following way:

glossary.definition: a part of the production system that contains a set of production rules and may include other components such as external applications and developer interface.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge base typically includes many production rules interrelated by sharing knowledge: knowledge units in the THEN part of one rule make the IF part of another rule (Mallach, 1994). Figure 1 demonstrates five core components of a rule-based expert system (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011): knowledge base, database or working memory, inference engine, explanation facilities, and user interface. However, additional components, such as external applications and developer interface, can be added as well.

glossary.definition: The processing mechanism of knowledge stored in the knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge is stored in the knowledge base, but its processing mechanism is the inference engine.

glossary.definition: A system that stores unrelated facts, unlike a knowledge base which stores relations among facts.
glossary.sentence: The database stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students. The knowledge base stores a representation not only of facts but also of relations among them.

glossary.definition: A specialist who knows methods and tools of artificial intelligence and can describe and represent knowledge in a knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base.

glossary.definition: An individual with deep knowledge and substantial practical experience in a particular domain.
glossary.sentence: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain.

glossary.definition: An automated system used by domain experts or knowledge engineers to describe knowledge and build a knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base.

glossary.definition: A knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialized domain at a level similar to human experts.
glossary.sentence: Intelligent computer systems that emulate the human expert’s methodology and performance are called expert systems (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: A set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge representation schemas are means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge.

glossary.definition: The average time taken to respond to a question.
glossary.sentence: the mean reaction time to the question “Can a canary bird fly?” is higher than the mean reaction time to the question “Can a canary bird sing?”

glossary.definition: A way of organizing knowledge where general features are associated with broader concepts and specific features with narrower concepts.
glossary.sentence: people do not store all features of a specific bird, but they organize knowledge about birds in a hierarchical manner: general features characterizing all birds are associated with a concept “Bird”

glossary.definition: Knowledge that is stored at the level of a specific concept rather than being generalized.
glossary.sentence: exceptional knowledge is stored at the level of a concept it belongs to.

glossary.definition: A knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph with nodes representing concepts and arcs representing relations between them.
glossary.sentence: Collins and Quillian created a semantic network that is displayed in Figure 4.

glossary.definition: The entities or ideas represented by nodes in a semantic network.
glossary.sentence: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts

glossary.definition: The lines connecting nodes in a semantic network that represent relations between concepts.
glossary.sentence: but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them

glossary.definition: A hierarchical relation showing that one concept is a type of another broader concept.
glossary.sentence: A relation “subclass-class” (also “is a subclass of”, “is a subset of”, “is a type of” or “is a kind of”) is represented by a title of “is a”.

glossary.definition: A hierarchical relation showing that an instance belongs to a particular class or category.
glossary.sentence: A relation “instance-class/set/type/kind” is represented by the title of “is an instance/example of”.

glossary.definition: A relation showing that one object is part of another whole object.
glossary.sentence: A relation “part-whole” is represented by the title of “Part of”.

glossary.definition: A relation showing that an object has a particular property or characteristic.
glossary.sentence: A relation “object-property” is represented by the title of “property/has a property of”.

glossary.definition: A relation showing the specific value of a property for an object.
glossary.sentence: A relation “property-value” is represented by the title of “value”.

glossary.definition: Relations that specify any other kind of relations not covered by predefined types, such as 'like', 'own', or 'travel'.
glossary.sentence: Linguistic relations (Fig.11). They allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations.

glossary.definition: The process by which properties defined in a class or subclass frame are automatically included in instance frames.
glossary.sentence: If necessary knowledge is not found, then the inference engine can apply the steps specified below and stop when the necessary knowledge is found: perform inheritance (if it is possible );

glossary.definition: Knowledge about facts, objects, and concepts; it does not include procedural or other types of knowledge.
glossary.sentence: they represent only declarative knowledge (knowledge about facts, objects, and concepts )

glossary.definition: A precise and unambiguous definition of the meaning of a representation or language.
glossary.sentence: they do not have well-defined formal semantics and can introduce ambiguity both during the knowledge representation when the same unit of knowledge can be represented in different ways, and during the interpretation of a semantic network, when the content represented can be interpreted in different ways;

glossary.definition: Schemas that use graphs to represent knowledge, similar to semantic networks.
glossary.sentence: Structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge.

glossary.definition: not represented explicitly by arcs as in semantic networks, but through a referencing principle where one node references another node by its name
glossary.sentence: relations are not represented explicitly by arcs at it was done in semantic networks, but a referencing principle is used when one node references another node by its name.

glossary.definition: a data-structure for representing a stereotyped situation (Minsky, 1975) or typical knowledge about an object/concept (Callan, 2003; Negnevitsky , 2011) or information relevant to stereotyped entities (Luger, 2009).
glossary.sentence: A frame is “a data-structure for representing a stereotyped situation” (Minsky, 1975). This means that an individual has experienced some sort of situation in the past. He/she has structured his/her knowledge of the situation and kept it in his/her memory. In a new situation that differs from the past, the person supplements or changes the previously acquired knowledge, for example, a person has already acquired higher education at Riga Technical University (RTU) and has knowledge of what it means to study at university; after several years, he/she has decided to acquire a second higher education in Sorbonne. There is, of course, a certain difference between RTU and Sorbonne, but the person will not have to re-establish an understanding of what the university is. A frame is “a data-structure for representing a stereotyped situation” (Minsky, 1975). In other words, it is a data structure that represents typical knowledge about an object/concept (Callan, 2003; Negnevitsky , 2011) or, in other words, information relevant to stereotyped entities (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: a remembered framework adapted to fit reality by changing details as necessary (Minsky, 1975)
glossary.sentence: When one encounters a new situation (or makes a substantial change in one's view of the present problem) one selects from memory a structure called a Frame. This is a remembered framework to be adapted to fit reality by changing details as necessary.” (Minsky, 1975).

glossary.definition: properties of objects/concepts in frames that have values which can be symbolic, numerical, boolean, empty, or contain a title of another frame (Jones, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992)
glossary.sentence: Slots are properties of objects/concepts (Jones, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992). Each property has a value that can be represented as a symbolic, numerical or boolean value, as well as it can be empty or can contain a title of another frame.

glossary.definition: The value stored in a slot of a frame.
glossary.sentence: Demons are triggered automatically when a slot value is accessed (Mallach, 1994). Thus, demons control actions for slot-level manipulations (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: an extension to the slot-value structure of frames representing extended knowledge about slot values and telling the inference engine how to process the property of the object (Negnevitsky , 2011)
glossary.sentence: Facets are an extension to the slot-value structure of frames as they represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: An expert system that uses frames to represent knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Demons implement actions in a frame-based expert system (Jones, 2009; Luger,2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992).

glossary.definition: a type of frame that contains common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts and helps represent problems in abstract form (Negnevitsky , 2011)
glossary.sentence: A class/generic frame. It contains common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts. The class frames help us to represent a problem in an abstract form ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A frame that represents a specific instance of a class or subclass.
glossary.sentence: Demons are usually defined in class and sub-class frames and then inherited and executed in instance frames. Object-oriented frames are acquired when demons are assigned to frames.

glossary.definition: a method used to relate two frames by using a unique title of one frame as a slot value in another frame (Negnevitsky , 2011)
glossary.sentence: A referencing principle is used to relate two frames. This means that a unique title of one frame is used as a slot value in another frame (Figure 7). Therefore, frames are related only by slot values, not by slot titles , because slot titles explain what kind of slot values is stored in the frame.

glossary.definition: a type of relation that relates a sub-class frame with a class frame or an instance frame with a sub-class/class frame, allowing hierarchies of frames (Negnevitsky , 2011)
glossary.sentence: Generalization relation . It relates a sub-class frame with a class frame or an instance frame with a sub-class/class frame. It allows making hierarchies of frames where the most general frame is located at the highest level and the most specific ones – at the lower levels. In different shells for creating frame-based knowledge bases, this type of relationship can be represented by different slots, like “Is-a”, “a-kind-of”, “class”, and others. Figure 8 demonstrates an example of the generalization relation between a sub-class Man and a class Human, and an instance John and a sub-class Man.

glossary.definition: a type of relation that allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts by associating frames corresponding to components with a frame representing the object as a whole (Negnevitsky , 2011)
glossary.sentence: Aggregation relation . It allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts (Figure 9). In other words, frames corresponding to the components of an object are associated with a frame representing the object as a whole . In different shells for creating frame-based knowledge bases, this type of relationship can be represented by slot titles “a-part-of” or “part-whole”.

glossary.definition: a type of relation that allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle (Negnevitsky , 2011)
glossary.sentence: Association or semantic relation . It allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle (Figure 10).

glossary.definition: a feature that allows assigning procedures or methods to slots in frames, which can calculate slot values when needed (Negnevitsky , 2011)
glossary.sentence: A slot can have a procedural attachment ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system.
glossary.sentence: In general, it is possible to add regular procedures and/or demons. A regular procedure is a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: Demons implement actions in a frame-based expert system (Jones, 2009; Luger,2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992). A demon is a procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: A frame that defines a class of objects and their common properties.
glossary.sentence: Demons are usually defined in class and sub-class frames and then inherited and executed in instance frames. Object-oriented frames are acquired when demons are assigned to frames.

glossary.definition: A frame that defines a subclass of objects and inherits properties from its parent class frame.
glossary.sentence: Demons are usually defined in class and sub-class frames and then inherited and executed in instance frames. Object-oriented frames are acquired when demons are assigned to frames.

glossary.definition: Predefined demons that are commonly used in frame-based knowledge bases.
glossary.sentence: There are standard demons defined for frames, although different shells for creating frame-based knowledge bases can offer a slightly different set of demons with different titles.

glossary.definition: A demon that is triggered when the slot value is unspecified and needs to be calculated or set.
glossary.sentence: The common demons are: IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it;

glossary.definition: A demon that is triggered when a slot value changes.
glossary.sentence: The common demons are: IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it; IF-CHANGED – it is triggered when a slot value changes;

glossary.definition: A demon that is triggered when a slot value is added to the frame.
glossary.sentence: The common demons are: IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it; IF-CHANGED – it is triggered when a slot value changes; IF-ADDED – it is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame;

glossary.definition: A demon that is triggered when a slot value is deleted from the frame.
glossary.sentence: The common demons are: IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it; IF-CHANGED – it is triggered when a slot value changes; IF-ADDED – it is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame; IF-REMOVED – it is triggered when a slot value is deleted.

glossary.definition: The code that describes what happens when a demon is called.
glossary.sentence: Although the demons are triggered automatically, their executable part (the code describing what will happen when the demon is called) should be developed by the developer of the intelligent computer system.

glossary.definition: The relationships between frames that are defined within a specific context or domain.
glossary.sentence: explore the local relations with other frames and try to get knowledge from them.

glossary.definition: A slot in a class frame that defines properties inherited by instance frames.
glossary.sentence: The generalization relation is defined between TreeAlex-Person , BrownLucie-Person , Tom-Cat , Rex-Dog , Cat-Animal , Dog-Animal . It is represented as a Class slot in the sub-class frames and instance frames.

glossary.definition: The relationships between frames that are not based on inheritance but on other associations or connections.
glossary.sentence: The frame system also represents the following associative relations between frames: BrownLucie - Tom , the relation Owns , and Tom- BrownLucie , the relation Animal owner;

glossary.definition: A knowledge base that uses frames to represent and organize knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Figure 13 represents a frame-based knowledge base that stores knowledge about persons and their home animals like cats and dogs.

glossary.definition: An intelligent computer system that can combine both search directions and switch between data-driven and goal-driven searches to maximize the use of new information in problem-solving.
glossary.sentence: It is possible to combine both search directions in a rule-based expert system ( Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992).

glossary.definition: A type of rule that specifies a condition and an action to be taken if the condition is met.
glossary.sentence: Production rules and production systems as their practical application are an example of procedural knowledge representation schemas.

glossary.definition: The part of a production rule that specifies when the rule should be triggered.
glossary.sentence: A production rule is presented in the following way (Callan, 2003; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): IF condition THEN consequence - action/statement/conclusion

glossary.definition: The part of a production rule that specifies what action should be taken if the condition is met.
glossary.sentence: A production rule is presented in the following way (Callan, 2003; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): IF condition THEN consequence - action/statement/conclusion

glossary.definition: The process of deriving specific conclusions from general principles or premises.
glossary.sentence: A production rule is presented in the following way (Callan, 2003; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): IF condition THEN consequence - action/statement/conclusion

glossary.definition: Frames that are organized in a hierarchical structure based on object-oriented principles.
glossary.sentence: Demons are usually defined in class and sub-class frames and then inherited and executed in instance frames. Object-oriented frames are acquired when demons are assigned to frames.

glossary.definition: rule-based expert systems that consist of three parts: a knowledge base, working memory, and inference engine.
glossary.sentence: Production systems are rule-based expert systems that consist of three parts (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2005; Negnevitsky , 2011): a knowledge base that contains a set of production rules; a working memory; an inference engine.

glossary.definition: The memory where facts are stored with time tags attached to them, used in conflict resolution.
glossary.sentence: This method relies on time tags attached to each fact in the working memory; thus, in the conflict set, the expert system first fires the rule whose antecedent uses the data most recently added to the working memory (Negnevitsky, 2011).

glossary.definition: components of an expert system that allow it to explain its reasoning and justify the output.
glossary.sentence: Explanation facilities are essential for any expert system because an expert system provides the user with a recommendation, plan, advice, conclusion, or any other result. However, to accept the system’s recommendation, the human expert should be aware of how a particular conclusion was reached, what was the line of reasoning and whether it is valid or not (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011). Thus, an expert system should explain its reasoning and justify the output ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: a component of an expert system that allows the user to interact with the system through a question-and-answer, menu-driven, natural language, or graphical interface.
glossary.sentence: The user interacts with the system through a user interface designed as a question-and-answer, menu-driven, natural language, or graphical interface (Luger, 2009). In any case, the communication with the user usually is very intensive, demanding from the user to supply information that is needed for reasoning.

glossary.definition: additional components of an expert system that may allow it to work with external data files and program results.
glossary.sentence: Additional components, such as external databases and programs , may allow an expert system to work with external data files and program results while the developer interface is intended to edit rules in the knowledge base (add new rules as well as modify and delete existing rules) and to check the performance of the expert system after updating the rules ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: additional components of an expert system that are intended to edit rules in the knowledge base and to check the performance of the expert system after updating the rules.
glossary.sentence: Additional components, such as external databases and programs , may allow an expert system to work with external data files and program results while the developer interface is intended to edit rules in the knowledge base (add new rules as well as modify and delete existing rules) and to check the performance of the expert system after updating the rules ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: a description of the current state of the problem-solving process stored in the working memory.
glossary.sentence: The working memory contains a description of the current state of the problem-solving process (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009) that is called a pattern .

glossary.definition: the 'Recognize-act' cycle performed by the inference engine to link rules in the knowledge base with facts in the working memory.
glossary.sentence: The inference engine performs the control cycle “Recognize-act” (Luger, 2009) to link the rules given in the knowledge base with the facts provided in the working memory (Negnevitsky, 2011). Thus, the inference engine carries out the reasoning by interpreting and applying the rules whereby the rule-based expert system reaches a solution (Hopgood, 2001; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: The method of choosing one rule to fire from the set of enabled rules (the conflict set).
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: a strategy used to select one rule from a set of enabled rules in the conflict set.
glossary.sentence: The selection of one rule from the conflict set is called conflict resolution . For this purpose, a conflict resolution strategy is used; one of the enabled rules is fired, and it changes the content of the working memory because its IF or THEN part is added to the working memory (depending on the search direction ); the control cycle repeats with the modified working memory; searching terminates when there are no more rules that match the content of the working memory.

glossary.definition: a technique for gathering information and inferring from it whatever can be inferred, typically used in problems of interpretation where most data are initially given.
glossary.sentence: Forward chaining is a technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred, typically used in problems of interpretation where most data are initially given.

glossary.definition: a process in rule-based expert systems that begins with a goal (a hypothetical solution) and attempts to find evidence to prove it.
glossary.sentence: In backward chaining , an expert system has the goal (a hypothetical solution), and the inference engine attempts to find the evidence to prove it ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: not found in the text.
An inference engine chosen when domain experts first gather information and then infer from it.
glossary.sentence: If a domain expert first needs to gather some information and then tries to infer from it whatever can be inferred, choose the forward chaining inference engine.

glossary.definition: An inference engine chosen when domain experts start with a hypothetical solution and attempt to find facts to prove it.
glossary.sentence: if your domain expert begins with a hypothetical solution and then attempts to find facts to prove it, choose the backward chaining inference engine.

glossary.definition: Rules used by human experts that specify conditions in a certain order and are applied in a certain order, determining successful problem-solving.
glossary.sentence: It is well known that problem domain experts use conditions specified in rules in a certain order and also apply rules in a certain order.

glossary.definition: The set of enabled rules from which one rule is chosen to fire during conflict resolution.
glossary.sentence: from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A method of firing more specific rules based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one.
glossary.sentence: This method is also known as the longest matching strategy: it is based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one ( Negnevitsky , 2011).
